title: LakeBank End-to-End Flow
course: Agentic Development with GitHub Copilot CLI, LakeBank, SyteLine, and MCP
prerequisites: Learner can start a safe GitHub Copilot CLI session.
summary: LakeBank is your product-repo workflow for agentic development.
Module 3 established activation readiness and created the handoff artifact (`memory-bank/repo-classification.md`).
Module 11 owns full end-to-end LakeBank execution after activation:
In this module, you run the complete LakeBank flow in a controlled way.
- conventions for writing durable repo knowledge
The goal of this module is to execute LakeBank safely without polluting memory, bypassing skills, or drifting into uncontrolled implementation.

This module focuses on reading LakeBank guidance, using it to shape the workflow, and identifying memory candidates. Actual memory-bank updates should only happen when the LakeBank workflow explicitly allows them and after human review.
whyItMatters: GitHub Copilot supports several customization primitives that are relevant to LakeBank-style workflows.
GitHub's custom instructions documentation describes custom instructions as a way to give Copilot additional context about how to understand a project and how to build, test, and validate changes.
GitHub's agent skills documentation describes skills as folders of instructions, scripts, and resources that Copilot can load when relevant for specialized tasks.

GitHub's Awesome Copilot repository is a community collection of custom agents, instructions, skills, hooks, workflows, and plugins. This provides a useful comparison point for how LakeBank and SyteLine plugin repos package internal knowledge.

Memory-bank systems such as Cline's Memory Bank show a related pattern: structured markdown files preserve project context across sessions. LakeBank is your team's productized version of that idea for product repos.

LakeBank matters because product repos contain durable product knowledge, risky legacy areas, build/test conventions, and team-specific workflows that should not need to be rediscovered from scratch every time.
If developers write bad memory, ignore LakeBank skills, or mix task-specific notes into durable repo memory, future agent work becomes worse.
howItFits: Earlier modules in the active sequence taught the fundamentals workflow and specialization prerequisites.
Module 3 made the LakeBank activation decision and produced the activation handoff artifact.
Module 11 is the owner of full LakeBank end-to-end execution.
learningObjectives: Execute the full LakeBank end-to-end flow after activation.
Explain what makes a product repo LakeBank-enabled.
Identify LakeBank instructions, skills, plugin references, and memory-bank files.
Explain how LakeBank changes the agentic development workflow.
Use LakeBank memory as context before research and planning.
Identify durable memory candidates without updating memory too early.
Explain when a LakeBank skill should be used.
Explain when SyteLine guidance may be referenced through LakeBank.
Avoid treating older product repos as LakeBank repos.
Create a LakeBank workflow assessment artifact.
Create a LakeBank memory candidates artifact.
Decide whether a task is ready for LakeBank-guided research, planning, implementation, or memory update review.
keyConceptsMarkdown: ### Concept 1: LakeBank is a workflow, not just files
A LakeBank-enabled repo is not simply a repo with a memory-bank folder.
It is a product repo that uses shared LakeBank guidance to shape how Copilot should work.
- references to SyteLine plugin guidance when applicable
Developers should treat LakeBank as the product-repo operating model for AI-assisted development.
### Concept 2: LakeBank should be detected by evidence
Do not assume a repo has LakeBank.
A repo should be classified as LakeBank-enabled only if there is evidence.
When a `memory-bank/` folder is present, look for files such as:
memory-bank/progress.md              ← current and archived task history
memory-bank/systemPatterns.md        ← stable architecture patterns and principles
If evidence is missing or partial, classify cautiously and ask for human review.
### Concept 3: LakeBank memory is durable memory
LakeBank memory is for durable repo/product knowledge.
### Concept 4: `memory-bank` is still needed in LakeBank repos
LakeBank does not replace task artifacts.
Even in LakeBank-enabled repos, use `memory-bank` for:
LakeBank memory is for durable learning.
`memory-bank` is for task execution and evidence.
GitHub describes agent skills as folders of instructions, scripts, and resources that Copilot can load when relevant.
LakeBank skills should be used when the task matches a reusable workflow.
Examples of possible LakeBank skill categories:
A developer should not reinvent a workflow in a one-off prompt when a LakeBank skill already exists.
### Concept 6: The install skill is special
LakeBank and SyteLine plugins have install skills that add instructions to repos to point to files in those plugins.
- do not hand-edit repo guidance unless the workflow allows it
- do not remove install skill output without review
- do not install LakeBank into repos that should intentionally remain without LakeBank
The install skill is part of repo enablement, not normal task work.
### Concept 7: LakeBank may reference SyteLine guidance
You said the SyteLine GitHub Copilot plugin is also referenced by LakeBank.
That means a LakeBank-enabled product repo may point developers toward SyteLine-specific guidance when the task touches SyteLine concepts.
- Is this product work or SyteLine-specific work?
- Does LakeBank point to SyteLine skill files?
- Is the repo a product repo or a customer modification repo?
- Does this task require SyteLine domain validation?
- Should this be handled by the LakeBank workflow, SyteLine workflow, or both?
Do not treat SyteLine guidance as present unless evidence exists.
### Concept 8: Memory updates should be candidates before they are commits
During work, Copilot may identify durable learnings.
Do not update LakeBank memory immediately unless the workflow explicitly calls for it.
Only after review should memory-bank files be updated.
### Concept 9: LakeBank should reduce tribal knowledge
LakeBank should capture knowledge that otherwise lives only in a senior developer's head.
- "This workflow affects costing and support triage."
- "This subsystem uses this non-obvious service boundary."
- "These tests are the safest validation path for this feature area."
- "This legacy area should use characterization tests first."
- "This configuration setting changes behavior in this workflow."
LakeBank should not capture personal opinions, temporary guesses, or unsupported claims.
Use this workflow in LakeBank-enabled product repos.
7. Review the plan with Rubber Duck and personas.
14. Prepare PR summary and support/docs handoff.

Discuss why using the wrong workflow is risky.
Run this in GitHub Copilot CLI:

- You may inspect files and folders.

lab.title: Apply the LakeBank workflow in a product repo
beforeYouStart: I reviewed `memory-bank/repo-classification.md` from Module 3 activation handoff.
I opened a terminal in a product repo believed to have LakeBank.
I checked the working tree status.
I cleared stale context or started a fresh session.
I will verify LakeBank evidence rather than assume it.
I will not replace repo instructions.
I will not install or modify plugins.
I will not modify MCP configuration.
I will keep task-specific notes in `memory-bank`.

prompts.label: Lab prompt
promptText: We are practicing the LakeBank workflow for an internal agentic development course.
- Do not add, remove, or change MCP configuration.
- Do not create a new memory bank.
- You may inspect files and folders.
Use these files as supporting context if they exist:
First, inspect this repo for LakeBank evidence.
Include status, repo path if known, branch if known, source change permission, and memory update permission.
Confirm activation handoff evidence from Module 3 and additional evidence found in this repo.
List instruction files, plugin references, install skill output, and docs that should guide Copilot.
List relevant LakeBank skills and what they appear to do. Do not invent skills.
List memory-bank files found and their apparent purpose.
List any SyteLine guidance referenced through LakeBank. If none is found, say so.
List which memory-bank artifacts should be used for task-specific research, planning, review, testing, implementation, and PR summaries.
Summarize how memory updates should be handled based on evidence. If unclear, say so.
Describe the correct workflow for future work in this repo.
Summarize what changed in this workflow assessment and why this matters for future work.
Explain why the selected LakeBank workflow is appropriate.
List evidence links that support the decision and recommendations.
State the top risk and required mitigation or reviewer.
Tell reviewers where to focus first.
Include status, repo path if known, branch if known, and whether memory updates are allowed now.
List possible durable learnings discovered from existing memory-bank artifacts or LakeBank inspection.
If there are no good memory candidates, say so.
- Do not treat temporary memory-bank notes as durable memory.
- Do not include secrets or customer-sensitive data.

prompts.label: Optional LakeBank-guided implementation prompt
promptText: We are doing LakeBank-guided controlled implementation.
Use these files as required context:
Implement only the approved phase from the plan.
- Stop if LakeBank guidance conflicts with the plan.

prompts.label: Optional memory candidate review prompt
promptText: Review @memory-bank/lakebank/lakebank-memory-candidates.md as a skeptical senior developer.

expectedContents: why this workflow decision was made

verificationChecklist: Did Copilot honor Module 3 activation handoff evidence?
Did Copilot find real LakeBank evidence?
Did Copilot avoid inventing skills or memory files?
Did Copilot identify memory-bank structure if present?
Did Copilot identify SyteLine references through LakeBank if present?
Did Copilot keep task artifacts in `memory-bank`?
Did Copilot avoid updating memory-bank files?
Did Copilot avoid repo instruction replacement?
Did Copilot correctly identify LakeBank plugin-related evidence while avoiding plugin or MCP configuration changes?
Did Copilot avoid source/test changes unless optional implementation was explicitly approved?
Did Copilot apply prohibited actions correctly in the recommended workflow?
Did Copilot recommend the correct LakeBank workflow?
Did Copilot include what changed, why, evidence links, and risk note?
Did Copilot include requested review focus for delivery handoff?
Did Copilot create memory candidates only as candidates?
Did Copilot include evidence and sensitivity checks for memory candidates?
Did Copilot label assumptions and unknowns?

evidence.summaryPrompt: Write 3-5 sentences describing what changed, why, key evidence, risk note, and how LakeBank should guide next work.
reflectionQuestions: What evidence showed that this repo is LakeBank-enabled?
What LakeBank guidance files or instruction pointers did Copilot find?
What LakeBank skills did Copilot find?
What memory-bank files did Copilot find?
What should stay in `memory-bank` instead of memory bank?
What must be reviewed before any memory update?
Did Copilot make any unsupported LakeBank assumptions?

skillCheck.question: What makes a repo LakeBank-enabled?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: A repo should be considered LakeBank-enabled only when evidence exists, such as LakeBank plugin references, install skill output, instruction pointers, LakeBank skills, memory-bank files, or docs that describe the LakeBank workflow.

skillCheck.question: What is the difference between LakeBank memory and `memory-bank` artifacts?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: LakeBank memory is durable, verified repo/product knowledge intended to help future work. `memory-bank` artifacts are task-specific working notes such as research, plans, reviews, tests, implementation notes, and PR summaries.

skillCheck.question: When should a LakeBank skill be used?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: A LakeBank skill should be used when the task matches a reusable LakeBank workflow, such as memory update, product workflow research, support triage, safe legacy change, test strategy, or PR summary generation.

skillCheck.question: What should happen when Copilot finds a possible durable learning during a task?
Why is LakeBank dangerous if used poorly?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Bad memory or incorrect guidance can mislead future agents and developers. Unverified guesses, customer-sensitive information, story-specific details, or stale facts in durable memory can cause future mistakes.

skillCheck.question: How can LakeBank reference SyteLine guidance?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: LakeBank may point to SyteLine plugin files or skills when product work touches SyteLine concepts. Developers should verify the references and use SyteLine guidance only when evidence shows it applies.

summaryMarkdown: In this module, you learned how to run the full LakeBank end-to-end workflow after activation.
- LakeBank is a product-repo workflow, not just a folder
- LakeBank must be detected by evidence
- `memory-bank` remains the place for task artifacts
- LakeBank skills should be used for reusable workflows
- install skills are special and should not be bypassed casually
- memory updates should start as candidates before becoming durable memory
- LakeBank should reduce tribal knowledge without preserving bad assumptions
- Module 11 owns full LakeBank execution scope
LakeBank should make product-repo agentic development safer, more consistent, and more repeatable.
readinessChecklist: I can explain what LakeBank is.
I can identify LakeBank evidence in a repo.
I can identify LakeBank guidance files.
I can explain how LakeBank changes the workflow.
I can explain why `memory-bank` is still needed.
I can identify memory candidates without updating memory.
I can explain when SyteLine guidance may be relevant through LakeBank.
commonMistakes: Assuming a repo has LakeBank without evidence.
Treating every product repo as LakeBank-enabled.
Using one-off prompts when a skill exists.
Putting task research into durable memory.
Putting customer-sensitive details into shared memory.
Treating memory candidates as approved memory updates.
Skipping human review before memory changes.
